mes_erp.title: MES / ERP
mes_erp.summary: Incumbent MES vendors are racing to bolt on AI and "composable" app layers while customers push back on 12-18 month deployments. The center of gravity is shifting from records-of-work to flexible, app-based execution.

happening.product: SAP launched DM Cloud with low-code extensions; Plex pushed an AI co-pilot for production reporting; Critical Manufacturing rolled out semiconductor-focused MES 11 with real-time AI quality.
happening.ai: All MES leaders are launching "generative co-pilots" for shift handover, work-order summarisation, and exception triage — Siemens Industrial Copilot, SAP Joule for manufacturing, Oracle's Fusion AI Agents.
happening.sales: Long deal cycles (9-15 months) remain. Vendors increasingly bundle MES into ERP transformation deals to dodge standalone scrutiny. Aerospace + life sciences seeing the most new logos.
happening.pricing: Implementation-heavy pricing ($500K–$5M+ rollouts). Critical Manufacturing and First Resonance trying SaaS subscription pivots to win mid-market.

takeaways: Lead with "app layer, not rip-and-replace" in every SAP/Plex/Oracle compete deal — 60-90 day pilots beat 12-month MES rollouts.
Counter Industrial Copilot narrative: show Tulip's frontline-workflow AI in operator hands, not just IT dashboards.
Build a coexistence reference architecture for SAP S/4HANA + Tulip on the shop floor; arm AEs with the diagram.

connected_worker.title: Connected Worker
connected_worker.summary: The category is consolidating around AI-augmented work. Augmentir's GenAI lead and Poka's IFS acquisition are reshaping competitive dynamics. Tulip's composability and platform extensibility remain unmatched here.

happening.product: Augmentir doubled down on GenAI skills agents; Poka deeply integrated into IFS Cloud as "IFS Connected Worker"; Parsable launched no-code procedure builder; Dozuki added AI-generated SOPs from video.
happening.ai: Generative procedure authoring is table stakes now. Augmentir leads on AI-driven training; Poka uses GenAI for video summarisation; SwipeGuide adds AI-suggested next-best-steps.
happening.sales: Land-and-expand motions dominate (single plant pilots → enterprise). Augmentir winning mid-market with PLG; Poka leveraging IFS sales force for enterprise reach.
happening.pricing: Per-user pricing $30–$80/month/user. Augmentir and MaintainX disrupting with $20-tier mobile-first SKUs.

takeaways: Differentiate hard on "platform vs point solution" — Tulip is the only connected-worker player with full app composability.
Counter Augmentir's GenAI narrative with Tulip Frontline Copilot positioning + real customer outcomes.
Build a Poka-to-Tulip migration play for IFS customers wanting a more flexible app layer.

qms.title: Quality / QMS
qms.summary: QMS vendors are expanding into manufacturing execution (MasterControl Manufacturing Excellence, Sparta's TrackWise + Honeywell). The line between QMS and MES is blurring — opportunity for Tulip to own the workflow execution layer.

happening.product: MasterControl shipped Manufacturing Excellence GA expanding into shop-floor execution; Kneat dominates validation lifecycle; Sparta + Honeywell pushing TrackWise Digital deeper into operations.
happening.ai: AI-assisted CAPA root cause, automated deviation triage, predictive quality risk scoring. MasterControl AI for batch record review now in public beta.
happening.sales: Long, compliance-heavy sales cycles. Pharma/biotech overwhelmingly drives the category. Co-sell motions with regulatory consultants common.
happening.pricing: $100K–$1M+ annual enterprise QMS. Implementation 6-12 months. Validation costs add 30-50%.

takeaways: Position Tulip as the execution layer above QMS — "QMS owns the records, Tulip owns the work."
Build pre-validated Tulip apps for common GMP workflows to neutralise MasterControl's compliance moat.
Co-sell with Sparta/Kneat where possible — protect the deal, expand the footprint.

cmms.title: CMMS / EAM
cmms.summary: MaintainX is reshaping the category with PLG and a $400M+ valuation; legacy EAM (IBM Maximo, Fiix) is pivoting to AI-led predictive maintenance. The category overlaps with Tulip on frontline mobile work.

happening.product: MaintainX shipped procedure-based work orders + IoT sensor integrations; IBM watsonx for Maximo expanding predictive scope; Fiix added GenAI work order summaries.
happening.ai: Predictive maintenance + GenAI work order triage are the focus. MaintainX's mobile-first AI gaining real traction in mid-market.
happening.sales: MaintainX winning massively on bottoms-up PLG; Maximo retaining enterprise asset-heavy verticals. Limble carving the SMB niche.
happening.pricing: MaintainX $16-$49/user/month freemium; Maximo $300K+ enterprise; Fiix per-asset pricing.

takeaways: Position Tulip as the operator workflow layer above CMMS — connect maintenance into broader plant ops, not just asset records.
Build integration content for MaintainX + Tulip and Maximo + Tulip — own the "frontline app" persona.
Don't compete directly on asset records; compete on workflow flexibility.

scada_ot.title: SCADA / OT
scada_ot.summary: The SCADA/OT layer is fragmenting between data infrastructure (HighByte, Litmus, AWS) and execution platforms. Inductive Automation Ignition is the breakout darling. Tulip's opportunity: workflow outcomes on top of OT data.

happening.product: Inductive Automation Ignition 8.3 added enterprise features; HighByte 4.0 expanded UNS support; AVEVA fully transitioned PI System to cloud; AWS IoT SiteWise added Edge Manager.
happening.ai: AVEVA AI Assistant for process engineers; AWS Bedrock + IoT integration patterns; HighByte using LLMs for data modeling assistance.
happening.sales: Inductive wins on unlimited-license pricing; AVEVA strong in upstream energy; HighByte/Litmus carving the modern UNS niche. Most deals through SIs.
happening.pricing: Inductive ~$10K/server unlimited; AVEVA per-tag enterprise; HighByte starts $25K; AWS pay-per-use.

takeaways: Don't try to be SCADA. Partner deeply with Inductive, HighByte, and AVEVA — own the workflow layer that turns OT data into operator action.
Build certified integrations and joint reference architectures with the top 2-3 SCADA players.
Sales play: "You've invested in data infrastructure — Tulip is how you turn it into shop-floor outcomes."

analytics.title: Analytics / Manufacturing Intelligence
analytics.summary: Manufacturing analytics has split into infrastructure (Seeq, TrendMiner) and outcomes (Sight Machine, MachineMetrics). Customers increasingly buy outcomes, not dashboards. Tulip's opportunity: close the loop from insight to action.

happening.product: Seeq added generative formula authoring; TrendMiner expanded into batch + continuous hybrid; Sight Machine launched Foundation Model for Manufacturing; MachineMetrics added AI machine prognostics.
happening.ai: All vendors are launching "foundation models for manufacturing" — Sight Machine, Seeq, AVEVA. Real impact still emerging. Most are LLM wrappers + good UX.
happening.sales: Outcomes-based selling rising — vendors pitch "15% OEE uplift" not dashboards. Pricing-on-impact deals appearing.
happening.pricing: Seeq enterprise $100K-$500K; MachineMetrics per-machine; Sight Machine outcome-based pilots.

takeaways: Lead with "insights to action" — Tulip apps close the loop on Seeq/TrendMiner data in operator hands.
Partner motion with Seeq for process industries; compete with Sight Machine on workflow execution.
Avoid the dashboard war — be the action layer that makes analytics worth the cost.

ai_vision.title: AI Vision / Quality AI
ai_vision.summary: AI vision is hot — Cognex still leads on hardware-anchored deployments, Landing AI on bring-your-own-camera SaaS. Both are starting to ship workflow features that overlap with Tulip's execution layer.

happening.product: Cognex In-Sight L38 with GenAI labeling; Landing AI VisionAgent for production deployment; both adding low-code workflow trigger features.
happening.ai: Cognex EdgeLearning + foundation models for surface defect; Landing AI's Large Vision Models for few-shot inspection.
happening.sales: Cognex deep with automotive + electronics; Landing AI winning mid-market with $100/camera/month entry.
happening.pricing: Cognex hardware-bundled $5K-$50K per station; Landing AI SaaS $100-$500 per camera per month.

takeaways: Frame Tulip as the workflow layer around vision/AI inspection — co-sell where possible, displace where customers want one platform.
Build native integrations with Cognex (deepest installed base) and Landing AI; turn their detections into Tulip workflows.
Don't try to be a vision vendor — be the orchestrator of action when vision detects an issue.

platform.title: Low-Code Platform
platform.summary: General-purpose low-code platforms (Mendix, OutSystems, Microsoft Power Platform) are showing up in manufacturing app evaluations. Tulip's advantage: manufacturing-native primitives that horizontal platforms can't match.

happening.product: Mendix shipping AI-assisted app modeling; deeper Siemens Xcelerator integration; Power Platform deepening manufacturing templates with Copilot Studio.
happening.ai: All horizontal players adding AI-assisted app build (Mendix Maia, Power Platform Copilot). Productivity gains real but manufacturing context still shallow.
happening.sales: Mendix winning where customer already standardized on Siemens; Power Platform winning Microsoft-heavy accounts; rarely chosen for true plant-floor work.
happening.pricing: Mendix enterprise platform licensing $50K+; Power Platform per-user from $20/month.

takeaways: Differentiate on manufacturing-native primitives: connectors to IoT/PLC, operator UX, quality workflows that horizontal platforms can't ship in 3 months.
When competing vs Mendix or Power Platform, lead with "time-to-first-shop-floor-app" demo.
Coexistence story: use horizontal platform for IT apps, Tulip for plant-floor; reduce sales friction.
